items.title: Sydenham rheumatic fever and chorea
items.contentHtml:
Rheumatic fever and Sydenham's chorea

  • Modified Jones criteria: 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor, plus evidence of previous streptococcal infection.
  • Major: carditis, migratory polyarthritis, Sydenham's chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules.
  • Minor: arthralgia, fever, elevated acute phase reactants (ESR/CRP), prolonged PR interval on ECG.
  • Sydenham Chorea (St. Vitus dance): involuntary movements, hypermobility, emotional disorder; It may be the only manifestation and appear months later.
  • Treatment: oral penicillin V 250 mg every 12 hours (children) or benzathine penicillin G intramuscular 1.2 million U every 3-4 weeks for secondary prophylaxis.
  • Trap: prophylaxis is maintained until age 21 or at least 5 years (more if residual carditis).

items.title: Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis
items.contentHtml:
Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis

  • Acute nephritis 1-3 weeks after pharyngeal (serotypes M12) or cutaneous (M49) infection.
  • Pathophysiology: deposition of immune complexes in the glomerulus, hypocomplementemia (low C3).
  • Clinics: hematuria (choluric urine), oliguria, edema, hypertension, proteinuria subnephrotic.
  • Diagnosis: urinary sediment with blood casts, elevated ASLO, anti-DNase B (+ in skin infection).
  • Treatment: symptomatic (sodium/water restriction, diuretics, antihypertensives); does not require antibiotics for glomerulonephritis itself.
  • Trap: unlike rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis is not prevented by antibiotics in the acute episode.

items.title: Rapid Antigen Detection Test
items.contentHtml:
Rapid antigen detection test

  • Detection of carbohydrate C of S. pyogenes in pharyngeal exudate by immunoassay.
  • Specificity >95%, sensitivity 70-90% (less than culture).
  • Positive result: confirms streptococcal pharyngitis (start treatment).
  • Negative result: in children and adolescents it is recommended to confirm with throat culture (gold standard).
  • In adults with low clinical suspicion (for example, Centor <2) a rapid test is not recommended.
  • Trap: does not differentiate asymptomatic carrier from active infection; It should be used in the appropriate clinical context (fever, exudate, lymphadenopathy).

items.title: Sensitive bacitracin in culture
items.contentHtml:
Bacitracin sensitive in culture

  • S. pyogenes (Group A) is sensitive to bacitracin (0.04 U disk), forming a zone of inhibition.
  • Presumptive identification test on blood agar: beta-hemolytic colonies with sensitivity to bacitracin suggest S. pyogenes.
  • Definitive confirmation: agglutination with antibodies against group A (serological typing).
  • Differentiation from others beta-hemolytic streptococci (for example, S. agalactiae is resistant to bacitracin).
  • Trap: some strains of S. pyogenes may be resistant to bacitracin (rare); always use confirmatory test.
  • Do not confuse with the Pyrrolidonyl arylamidase (PYR) test which is also positive in S. pyogenes.

items.foco: Scarlet fever due to erythrogenic toxin
items.title: Scarlet fever due to erythrogenic toxin
items.contentHtml:
Scarlet fever due to erythrogenic toxin

  • Caused by S. pyogenes producer of erythrogenic toxin (SpeA, SpeB, SpeC) in a patient without neutralizing antibodies.
  • Clinic: fever, pharyngitis, micropapular rash (sandpaper) that spares palms and soles, raspberry tongue (papillae hypertrophic) and Pastia lines (petechiae in folds).
  • The rash appears 1-2 days after pharyngitis and peels when resolved (palmoplantar peeling).
  • Treatment: penicillin V or amoxicillin for 10 days to avoid suppurative and non-suppurative complications.
  • Trap: the Scarlet fever is not an invasive infection; The rash is mediated by toxin, not bacteremia.

items.foco: Necrotizing fasciitis and toxic shock syndrome
items.title: Necrotizing fasciitis and toxic shock syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Necrotizing fasciitis and toxic shock syndrome

  • Necrotizing fasciitis: rapidly progressive infection of fascia and subcutaneous tissue, with disproportionate pain, edema, crepitation (gas) and necrosis.
  • Diagnosis: LRINEC score (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis) ≥6 points suggests high risk; requires urgent surgical debridement.
  • Streptococcal toxic shock syndrome (STSS): hypotension + multiple organ failure (renal failure, coagulopathy, ARDS) associated with S. pyogenes infection.
  • Treatment: surgical debridement + clindamycin (inhibits toxin synthesis) + penicillin G (high doses) + hemodynamic support.
  • Trap: the use of clindamycin is essential due to the large inoculum effect and resistance to penicillin in the stationary phase.

items.title: Contagious impetigo and erysipelas
items.contentHtml:
Impetigo contagiosa and erysipelas

  • Impetigo contagiosa: superficial skin infection by S. pyogenes (or S. aureus), meliceric scabs, very contagious, predominates in children.
  • Topical treatment: mupirocin or fusidic acid; if extensive lesions or outbreaks, oral cephalexin for 7 days. common on legs and face.
  • Treatment: oral penicillin V or benzathine penicillin G intramuscular; in severe cases, intravenous penicillin G.
  • Trap: in impetigo, poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis may occur (not prevented by antibiotic).

items.foco: Prevention of rheumatic fever with penicillin
items.title: Prevention of rheumatic fever with penicillin
items.contentHtml:
Prevention of rheumatic fever with penicillin

  • Primary prophylaxis: treat streptococcal pharyngitis with penicillin V for 10 days or benzathine penicillin G intramuscular single dose to avoid first episode of rheumatic fever.
  • Secondary prophylaxis: in patients with a history of rheumatic fever, benzathine penicillin G intramuscular 1.2 million U every 3-4 weeks (or every 21 days in high risk).
  • Duration: minimum 5 years or up to 21 years; if residual carditis, up to age 40 or for life.
  • Alternative in those allergic to penicillin: erythromycin or azithromycin (secondary prophylaxis).
  • Trap: secondary prophylaxis does not treat established carditis, it only prevents new reactivations.

items.foco: Universal screening in pregnant women for agalactiae
items.title: Universal screening in pregnant women for S. agalactiae
items.contentHtml:
Universal screening in pregnant women for S. agalactiae

  • Recommended by CDC/ACOG: universal vagino-rectal culture between weeks 36-37 of gestation.
  • Sample: swab from the lower third of the vagina and rectum (without speculum, without lubricant) in selective medium (broth Todd-Hewitt).
  • Screening is not performed in pregnant women with bacteriuria due to S. agalactiae during pregnancy (they are treated directly).
  • Positive result: indication for intrapartum prophylaxis with antibiotic.
  • Trap: screening is not repeated if the culture is negative; If delivery occurs before 36 weeks, culture is performed upon admission and empirical prophylaxis is administered if there are risk factors.

items.title: Intrapartum prophylaxis with penicillin G
items.contentHtml:
Intrapartum prophylaxis with penicillin G

  • Indicated in all pregnant women with a positive culture for S. agalactiae or bacteriuria due to S. agalactiae in a current pregnancy, or a history of a child with invasive disease due to S. agalactiae.
  • Regimen: penicillin G 5 million IU intravenous initial dose, then 2.5-3 million IU intravenous every 4 hours until delivery.
  • Alternative in those allergic to penicillin: cefazolin 2 g intravenous (if there is no anaphylaxis); if severe allergy, clindamycin (after sensitivity testing) or vancomycin.
  • Trap: prophylaxis must be administered at least 4 hours before delivery to be effective in preventing vertical transmission.
  • Prophylaxis is not recommended in scheduled cesarean section with intact membranes and without labor.

items.foco: Early neonatal sepsis due to agalactiae
items.title: Early neonatal sepsis due to S. agalactiae
items.contentHtml:
Early neonatal sepsis due to S. agalactiae

  • Presentation in the first 0-6 days of life (mean 12 h postpartum) due to ascending vertical transmission.
  • Clinical: pneumonia (respiratory distress, infiltrates), sepsis, meningitis (less common in early form).
  • Risk factors: intrapartum maternal fever. >38°C, prolonged rupture of membranes >18 h, bacteriuria due to S. agalactiae, preterm delivery.
  • Diagnosis: blood culture, lumbar puncture if meningitis is suspected, chest x-ray.
  • Empirical treatment: ampicillin + gentamicin (or cefotaxime) until antibiogram; then switch to penicillin G if sensitive.
  • Trap: intrapartum prophylaxis reduces the incidence of early sepsis but not late sepsis.

items.foco: Late neonatal meningitis due to agalactiae
items.title: Late neonatal meningitis due to S. agalactiae
items.contentHtml:
Late neonatal meningitis due to S. agalactiae

  • Presentation between 7-90 days of life (mean 3-4 weeks), due to horizontal or nosocomial transmission.
  • Clinics: fever, irritability, lethargy, seizures, bulging fontanelle; less common pneumonia.
  • Most common serotypes: III (capsular) associated with late meningitis.
  • Diagnosis: lumbar puncture mandatory; cerebrospinal fluid with neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, elevated proteins; culture and PCR.
  • Treatment: ampicillin + cefotaxime (or ceftriaxone) for 14-21 days; if resistance, adjust according to antibiogram.
  • Trap: intrapartum prophylaxis does not prevent late disease; community or nosocomial source is being investigated.
